MR. DEEPESH MAJIGAMKAR

08 January 2026 18:00
To:
Deepesh Majigamkar <chartering@varitasnavigation.com>
Subject:
NSI Daily Market Report 08/01/2026
Hi Deepesh
We have opened flat with the market still looking for direction.
On Sunday, OPEC+ had decided to pause unwinding its voluntary cuts by a total of 2.9 million barrels per day over the next few months in order to keep that volume off the market through the first half of the year whilst concerns of an oversupply continues to heavily weigh on sentiment and oil prices. This reflects that the group is exerting more caution over ensuring the market is balanced as robust non-OPEC production growth continues to rise, despite the warnings of a global gut, which has put pressure on prices and created additional complications regarding the group\'s compliance. After the OPEC members confirmed their decisions at the Sunday meeting, the group had emphasised that their strategy will still be centrally based on flexibility in order to either extend or reverse additional voluntary adjustments in accordance to the market\'s current state. OPEC+ had also reiterated that they will enforce a collective commitment to full conformity with the Declaration of Cooperation, confirming that they will fully compensate any overproduction that has occurred since January 2024, as the Joint Ministerial Monitoring Committee (JMMC) will be responsible for making sure that compliance and compensation is fulfilled. Moreover, the group had suggested that they\'re considering to return the 1.65 million barrels per day voluntary production cuts, either in part or in full, but this is largely dependant on how the market conditions may evolve, noting that the cuts would only be made gradually. With this in mind, key OPEC+ producers - the UAE, Iraq, Kazakhstan and Oman - have all promised to improve their compliance, and will implement output cuts as the oil markets continue to suffer from the oversupply amid weakening demand growth. Until June, the four producers will cut output by a total of 829,000 barrels per day: Kazakhstan has pledged to increase its cut from 131,000 barrels per day to 669,000 barrels per day, Iraq will maintain its 100,000 barrels per day output cut by midyear, the UAE will boost its cut from 10,000 barrels per day to 55,000 barrels per day, and Oman will only cut by roughly 5,700 barrels per day through June. Meanwhile, it has been forecasted that global oil markets will continue being oversupplied in 2026 whilst oil demand growth is expected to remain at weaker levels, relative to historical averages. This is largely because of improving vehicle efficiency, rising adoption of electric/hybrid vehicles in major economies, and a more volatile macroeconomic backdrop, which have all contributed to towards the periodical inventory accumulation in global markets, including the higher visible stocks across some of Asia. Rising non-OPEC production is set to the lead the supply growth, as the United States, Brazil, Canada, Guyana, and Argentina have plans to bring new projects come online and improve operational efficiency. To note, US crude output is expected to remain near its record levels this year, after its production had hit an all-time high of 13.87 million barrels per day in October, due to production growth offshore the Gulf of Mexico and steady shale output - though, this was slightly offset by softer production in Texas.
As the US blockade on Venezuelan oil continues after the seizure and capture of Maduro, the South American\'s shipments to Asia are unsurprisingly at a standstill, with Venezuela’s top oil customer, China, currently receiving lower volumes of crude. Bloomberg reported, citing unnamed sources, that as Chinese oil buyers reduce their purchases, the blockade has caused Venezuelan oil prices to spike, the discount between Brent and Venezuela’s flagship Merey crude dropping from $15 per barrel in December to $13 per barrel this month. The US “oil quarantine” has stunted Venezuela’s state oil firm PDVSA to ship oil cargoes to Asia for nearly a week, showed shipping data that was reviewed by Reuters; Secretary of State Marco Rubio had stated earlier this week that the US naval blockade, that’s disrupting tanker traffic to and from Venezuela, isn’t going to listed anytime soon. Whereas Chevron is the only Western oil company that is currently authorized by the US Treasury to operate in Venezuela since it ships the to the US Gulf Coast - though this may change now that Maduro is out of leadership. However, Trump has claimed that the United States and Venezuela has already reached a deal, which Venezuelan authorities have agreed to export $2 billion worth of crude oil to the US. Reuters has noted that the mentioned agreement, and the new leadership - that\'s exhibiting compliance with the US - will potentially be able to increase the pressure on illicit Venezuelan shipments of cheap crude to China, on top of boosting heavy crude supply to the US Gulf Coast refineries. Venezuela has the largest proven oil reserves in the world that contains sour heavy crude, which is hard to procure from other sources. This deal would be very beneficial to the US Gulf Coast refineries since they are configured to process heavy crude, meaning that the agreement will strengthen supply security as they strongly rely on Venezuelan exports for that particular crude grade.  Moreover, it is no secret that the US is looking to gain access to Venezuela\'s crude, and it should also be noted Trump had said earlier this week that Venezuela would be "turning over" between 30 and 50 million barrels of oil to the United States, insisting that Venezuela’s interim Delcy Rodriguez has given the US, and private companies, “total access” to Venezuela\'s oil industry. US Interior Secretary Doug Burgum had told Fox News on Tuesday that “Venezuela has an opportunity now to actually have capital come in and rebuild their economy and take advantage... With American technology, American partnership, Venezuela can be transformed.” Although this will likely be beneficial for Venezuela\'s energy sector, on top of providing substantial amounts of oil and revenue for the United States, the prospect of additional barrels amid concerns of oversupply, has weighed down on oil prices and further depressed market sentiment.
